questions.question: The term 'accounting period' is used to refer to the
options: time span during which taxes are paid to the Inland Revenue Board
budget period, usually one year, relied on by the accountant
time span, usually one year covered by financial statement
period within which debtors are expected to settle accounts
questions.explanation: An accounting period is the time span, usually one year, covered by financial statements. It is also known as the financial year or fiscal year.
questions.hint: Think about the period for which financial statements are prepared.

questions.question: Assigning revenues to the accounting period in which goods were sold or services rendered and expenses incurred is known as
questions.explanation: The matching concept requires that revenues be matched with the expenses incurred to generate them in the same accounting period.
questions.hint: Match revenues with related expenses in the same period.

questions.question: The accounting convention which states that 'profit must not be recognized until realized while all losses should be adequately provided for' is termed
questions.explanation: The conservatism (prudence) convention states that anticipated losses should be recognized immediately, but anticipated profits should only be recognized when realized.
questions.hint: The principle of 'playing safe' - recognize losses early, profits late.

questions.question: Accounting information is used by investors and creditors of a company to predict
options: future cash flows of the company
future tax payments of the company
potential merger candidates for the company
appropriate remuneration for the company's staff
questions.explanation: Investors and creditors use accounting information to predict future cash flows, which helps them assess the company's ability to pay dividends and repay loans.
questions.hint: What would investors and lenders want to predict about a company's future?

questions.question: Antics Electronics Company recently bought six generators. Which of the following is the correct method of recording this transaction?
options: Debit generator account and credit cash account.
Debit purchases account and credit cash account.
Debit cash account and credit purchases account.
Debit cash account and credit generator account.
questions.explanation: When purchasing fixed assets (generators), the asset account (generator) is debited, and cash is credited (if paid immediately).
questions.hint: The receiver (generator) is debited, the giver (cash) is credited.

questions.question: When a business incurs labour cost in installing a fixed asset, the cost is treated as
questions.explanation: Labour costs incurred to install a fixed asset are capitalized as part of the asset's cost because they are necessary to bring the asset to its usable condition.
questions.hint: Costs that bring an asset to working condition are added to the asset value.

questions.question: What is the cardinal rule of the double entry system?
options: Debit the increasing account and credit the decreasing account.
Debit the receiving account and credit the giving account.
Debit the asset account and credit the liability account.
Debit the revenue account and credit the expenditure account.
questions.explanation: The cardinal rule of double entry is: Debit the receiver, credit the giver. This applies to personal accounts.
questions.hint: Who receives? Who gives?

questions.question: Malam Gambo bought a freezer for his shop costing N10,500. In recording, he debited office expenses account and credited the bank account. What book keeping error has he committed?
questions.explanation: Error of principle occurs when a transaction is recorded in the wrong class of account - here, a fixed asset (freezer) was treated as an expense (office expenses).
questions.hint: Is a freezer an asset or an expense?

questions.question: The use of the folio in the ledger is for
questions.explanation: The folio column is used for referencing - it shows the page number in the journal or other books where the transaction was originally recorded.
questions.hint: It helps you find the original entry in another book.

questions.question: Account of Tanko Nig Ltd. (Dr: 1/10 Bal b/f N5,000; Sales N10,000 | Cr: NIL). What is the closing balance of the account shown above?
questions.explanation: Total debit = N5,000 + N10,000 = N15,000. Total credit = N0. Closing balance = N15,000 debit.
questions.hint: Add all debits, subtract all credits.
questions.formula: Total Debits - Total Credits

tableData.description: T-account with debit side: 1/10 Bal b/f N5,000, Sales N10,000; credit side: NIL

questions.question: The suspense account is used to correct book keeping errors where the
options: error does not affect the agreement of the trial balance
questions.explanation: A suspense account is used when the nature of an error or the account affected is unknown at the time, or when the trial balance does not agree.
questions.hint: Used when you don't know where to put something temporarily.

questions.question: The discount column of a three column cashbook is not balanced off but periodically transferred to the discount accounts because discounts
options: are not important in cash book
do not form part of double entry
questions.explanation: Discount columns in the cash book are memorandum columns only. The actual double entry for discounts is completed when totals are posted to discount allowed and discount received accounts.
questions.hint: Discount columns are just for memoranda, not part of double entry.

questions.question: When is a petty cash account debited?
options: When the fund is established and every time money is spent
when the fund is established and every time it is replenished
when the fund is established and when the size of the float is decreased
Every time money is drawn from the petty cash
questions.explanation: The petty cash account is debited when the fund is first established and each time it is replenished (to restore the float to its original amount).
questions.hint: The account gets debited when money goes INTO petty cash.

questions.question: When preparing a bank reconciliation statement, which of the following is deducted from the balance per bank statement?
questions.explanation: Unpresented cheques (cheques issued but not yet presented to the bank for payment) are deducted from the bank statement balance because the bank has not yet reduced the balance.
questions.hint: Cheques you've written but the bank hasn't paid yet.

questions.question: The financial position of an organization at a particular time can be ascertained from the
options: statement of sources and application of funds
questions.explanation: The balance sheet shows the financial position of an organization at a specific point in time, presenting assets, liabilities, and equity.
questions.hint: Which statement is like a 'snapshot' of the business?

questions.question: Given: Purchases N20,000, Sales N40,000, Carriage inwards N5,000, Carriage outwards N5,000, Opening stock N10,000, Closing stock N5,000. What is the cost of goods sold?
questions.explanation: Cost of Goods Sold = Opening Stock + Purchases + Carriage Inwards - Closing Stock = N10,000 + N20,000 + N5,000 - N5,000 = N30,000.
questions.hint: COGS = Opening Stock + Purchases + Carriage Inwards - Closing Stock
questions.formula: Opening Stock + Purchases + Carriage Inwards - Closing Stock

questions.question: The net profit or loss for the year is determined in the profit and loss account after
options: deducting cost of goods sold from sales and adding administrative expenses
adding commissions received to gross profit
eliminating all expenses from gross profit and adding any other income
questions.explanation: Net profit is determined by deducting all expenses from gross profit and adding any other income (like commissions received, discounts received, etc.).
questions.hint: Gross Profit - Expenses + Other Income = Net Profit

questions.question: The following stock data is available: Opening balance (1/1/90): 3,000 units at N3.00 each; January purchases: 15,000 units at N3.50 each; February purchases: 25,000 units at N4.00 each; January sales: 13,000 units at N4.50 each; February sales: 28,000 units at N5.00 each. Using the LIFO (Last In First Out) method, determine the value of closing stock.
questions.explanation: LIFO assumes last items purchased are sold first. Total units available = 3,000 + 15,000 + 25,000 = 43,000 units. Total sales = 13,000 + 28,000 = 41,000 units. Closing stock = 2,000 units. Under LIFO, closing stock comes from earliest purchases: 2,000 units @ N3.00 = N6,000. (Note: The correct answer according to the source is N7,600, which may use a different LIFO application method - periodic vs perpetual.)
questions.hint: Under LIFO, closing stock comes from the oldest inventory layers.
questions.formula: Closing Stock = Units remaining × Cost per unit (from earliest layers under LIFO)

tableData.description: Stock data: Opening balance 3,000 units @ N3.00; Jan purchases 15,000 units @ N3.50; Feb purchases 25,000 units @ N4.00; Jan sales 13,000 units @ N4.50; Feb sales 28,000 units @ N5.00

questions.question: Using the same stock data (Opening: 3,000 units @ N3.00; Jan purchases: 15,000 @ N3.50; Feb purchases: 25,000 @ N4.00; Jan sales: 13,000 @ N4.50; Feb sales: 28,000 @ N5.00), what is the gross profit at the end of February if the closing stock were valued at N20,000?
questions.explanation: Sales revenue = (13,000 × N4.50) + (28,000 × N5.00) = N58,500 + N140,000 = N198,500. Cost of Goods Sold = Opening stock + Purchases - Closing stock = (N9,000 + N52,500 + N100,000) - N20,000 = N161,500 - N20,000 = N141,500. Gross Profit = Sales - COGS = N198,500 - N141,500 = N57,000.
questions.hint: Gross Profit = Sales - Cost of Goods Sold
questions.formula: Gross Profit = Sales - (Opening Stock + Purchases - Closing Stock)

tableData.description: Same stock data as question 18 with closing stock valued at N20,000

questions.question: A trial balance shows provision for doubtful debts, N190 and debtors, N6,000. It is required to write off further N320 as bad debts and maintain the provision at 2.5% of debtors. To do this, the profit and loss should show a
options: credit of N20 and a debit of N100
credit of N28 and a debit of N300
questions.explanation: After writing off N320 bad debt, debtors become N5,680. Required provision at 2.5% = N5,680 × 0.025 = N142. Existing provision was N190. Reduction in provision = N190 - N142 = N48 (credit to P&L). However, bad debt of N320 is debited to P&L. Net effect = N320 debit - N48 credit = N272 debit? The answer given is debit entry of N414, which suggests a different calculation approach.
questions.hint: Calculate new debtors after write-off, then new provision, then compare.
questions.formula: New Provision = (Debtors - Bad Debts Written Off) × Provision Rate

questions.question: If a bad debt previously written off is subsequently repaid, the amount collected is recorded as an
options: income in the profit and loss account
addition cash in the profit and loss account
questions.explanation: When a bad debt previously written off is recovered, it is treated as income in the profit and loss account (usually as 'Bad Debts Recovered' or 'Recovery of Bad Debts').
questions.hint: Recovery of a written-off bad debt is a gain to the business.

questions.question: Beginning and ending accounts receivable balances were N8,000 and N15,000 respectively. If collections from customers during the period were N36,000, then total sales on account would be
questions.explanation: Sales on account = Closing debtors + Collections - Opening debtors = N15,000 + N36,000 - N8,000 = N43,000.
questions.hint: Opening + Credit Sales - Collections = Closing
questions.formula: Credit Sales = Closing Debtors + Collections - Opening Debtors

questions.question: From which of the following sources are creditors control account most likely to be posted?
questions.explanation: The purchases journal (purchases day book) is the primary source for posting to the creditors control account, as it records all credit purchases.
questions.hint: Which journal records all credit purchases?

questions.question: Chukwuma Okeke did not keep his books based on the double entry principle. On 1 January 1993: Cash in hand N60, Bank overdraft N1,100, Debtor N7,240, Stock N27,450, Furnishings N2,000, Creditors N6,990. Compute the capital as at 1 January 1993.
questions.explanation: Capital = Total Assets - Total Liabilities. Assets = Cash N60 + Debtors N7,240 + Stock N27,450 + Furnishings N2,000 = N36,750. Liabilities = Bank overdraft N1,100 + Creditors N6,990 = N8,090. Capital = N36,750 - N8,090 = N28,660? The answer given is N29,800, suggesting different treatment of bank overdraft.
questions.hint: Capital = Assets - Liabilities
questions.formula: Capital = Total Assets - Total Liabilities

questions.question: Using the same information for Chukwuma Okeke: On 1 January 1993: Cash N60, Bank overdraft N1,100, Debtors N7,240, Stock N27,450, Furnishings N2,000, Creditors N6,990. As at 31 December 1993: Cash N40, Bank N1,050, Debtors N6,750, Stock N29,180, Furnishings N2,000, Creditors N6,090. Determine his net profit as at 31 December 1993.
questions.explanation: Closing capital = Assets at 31/12: Cash N40 + Bank N1,050 + Debtors N6,750 + Stock N29,180 + Furnishings N2,000 = N39,020. Liabilities: Creditors N6,090. Closing capital = N39,020 - N6,090 = N32,930. Opening capital (from Q24) = N29,800. Net profit = Closing capital - Opening capital = N32,930 - N29,800 = N3,130? The answer given is N2,200.
questions.hint: Net Profit = Closing Capital - Opening Capital (assuming no drawings or additional capital)
questions.formula: Net Profit = Closing Capital - Opening Capital

questions.question: Which of the following categories of labour cost will be classified as direct?
questions.explanation: Direct labour costs are those that can be directly traced to the production of goods. Factory workers' salaries are direct labour because they work directly on the products.
questions.hint: Who works directly on the products being made?

questions.question: The following inventory data is available: Beginning inventory: 20 units at N6 each = N120; Purchase No. 1: 30 units at N8 each = N240; Purchase No. 2: 10 units at N9 each = N90. Sales: Sale No. 1: 18 units; Sale No. 2: 20 units. Using the FIFO method of stock valuation, the ending inventory cost is
questions.explanation: Total units available = 20 + 30 + 10 = 60 units. Total sold = 18 + 20 = 38 units. Closing stock = 22 units. Under FIFO, remaining units come from latest purchases: 10 units @ N9 = N90, and 12 units @ N8 = N96. Total = N186? The answer given is N165, suggesting different sales quantities.
questions.hint: FIFO assumes first items purchased are sold first.
questions.formula: Closing stock value = Sum of remaining units × their respective costs (from latest layers under FIFO)

tableData.description: Beginning inventory: 20 units @ N6; Purchase 1: 30 units @ N8; Purchase 2: 10 units @ N9; Sale 1: 18 units; Sale 2: 20 units

questions.question: Using the same inventory data (Beginning: 20 units @ N6; Purchase 1: 30 units @ N8; Purchase 2: 10 units @ N9; Sale 1: 18 units; Sale 2: 20 units) and the LIFO inventory procedures, the ending inventory cost is
questions.explanation: Under LIFO, closing stock comes from earliest layers. 22 units remaining: 20 units from beginning @ N6 = N120, and 2 units from Purchase 1 @ N8 = N16. Total = N136. The answer given is N176.
questions.hint: LIFO means last in, first out - closing stock is from oldest inventory.
questions.formula: Closing stock value = Sum of remaining units × their respective costs (from earliest layers under LIFO)

questions.question: The major difference between the receipt and payment account and the income and expenditure account is that while the former
options: is kept by the treasurer, the latter is not
deals with all receipts and payments in the year regardless of the time it relates to while the latter is for just that year
is a T-account the latter is not
is not in the ledger, the latter is
questions.explanation: Receipts and Payments account is a cash-based summary of all cash transactions. Income and Expenditure account is an accrual-based account that matches income and expenses for the period.
questions.hint: Receipts & Payments = Cash basis; Income & Expenditure = Accrual basis

questions.question: The following information relates to a hockey club: Subscription owing at the beginning N40, Subscription owing at the end N35, Subscription received in advance at the beginning N15, Subscription received during the period N2,560. The amount to be shown in the income and expenditure account would be
questions.explanation: Subscription for I&E = Received + Opening arrears - Closing arrears - Opening advance + Closing advance = N2,560 + N40 - N35 - N15 + N0 = N2,550? The answer given is N2,570.
questions.hint: Subscription income = Cash received + Opening arrears - Closing arrears - Opening advance + Closing advance
questions.formula: I&E Subscription = Cash Received + Opening Arrears - Closing Arrears - Opening Advance + Closing Advance

questions.question: The net profit from the trading account of a non-profit making organization would be treated as income in the
questions.explanation: For non-profit organizations, the Income and Expenditure Account is equivalent to the Profit and Loss Account of a profit-making business. Trading profits are transferred to the Income and Expenditure Account.
questions.hint: Which account is equivalent to Profit & Loss for non-profits?

questions.question: Ada and Udo are lawyers who went into partnership as Ado & Co. Ada brought cash of N12,000, furnishing worth N18,000 and a motor vehicle worth N70,000. Udo brought cash N10,000, his building valued at N105,000 and a personal computer worth N13,000. What is the capital of Ado & Co.?
questions.explanation: Total capital = Ada's contribution (N12,000 + N18,000 + N70,000 = N100,000) + Udo's contribution (N10,000 + N105,000 + N13,000 = N128,000) = N228,000. The answer given is N250,000.
questions.hint: Add all assets contributed by both partners.
questions.formula: Total Capital = Sum of all partners' contributions

questions.question: Using the same partnership information (Ada: N100,000 total; Udo: N128,000 total), what is the profit sharing ratio if it is based on capital contributed by Ada and Udo?
questions.explanation: Ada's capital = N100,000, Udo's capital = N128,000. Ratio = 100,000:128,000 = Simplify dividing by 4,000 = 25:32 ≈ 1:1.28. The closest given is 1:2? The answer is 1:2.
questions.hint: Simplify the ratio by dividing both numbers by their greatest common divisor.
questions.formula: Ratio = Partner A Capital : Partner B Capital (simplified)

questions.question: The major distinguishing element between the final accounts of a partnership and a sole trader is the
questions.explanation: The Appropriation Account is unique to partnerships. It shows how the net profit is distributed among partners (salaries, interest on capital, profit sharing).
questions.hint: Which account shows how profit is shared among partners?

questions.question: Goodwill appears in the books of a business only if it has been
options: raised in connection with the admission of a new partner
raised to account for the true value of a business on the death of a partner
raised in order to prevent the balance sheet showing that the business is insolvent
questions.explanation: Under accounting conventions, goodwill is only recognized in the books when it has been purchased (acquired) for a price. Internally generated goodwill is not recognized.
questions.hint: Can you record self-generated goodwill?

questions.question: Kayode, Akpan and Kachalla are in partnership. Their respective capital accounts had the following balances: N40,000, N50,000 and N70,000. The partners agree to admit Wamo as a new partner with a one-fifth interest in the partnership capital in exchange for N50,000 cash. Wamo's equity in the resulting partnership is
questions.explanation: Total existing capital = N40,000 + N50,000 + N70,000 = N160,000. After admitting Wamo with 1/5 interest, total capital = N160,000 + N50,000 = N210,000. Wamo's equity = 1/5 × N210,000 = N42,000.
questions.hint: New partner's equity = Their share percentage × New total capital
questions.formula: New Partner's Equity = (New Partner's Share) × (Old Capital + New Partner's Contribution)

questions.question: Under which of the following conditions is a partnership dissolved?
options: Change of the partnership's head office
Purchase of a large quantity of fixed assets
Retirement of a manager who is not a partner
questions.explanation: Under the Partnership Act, the admission of a new partner dissolves the existing partnership and a new partnership is formed.
questions.hint: When does the partnership agreement change fundamentally?

questions.question: When shares issued are paid for, the accounting entry required is
options: debit bank or cash account and credit share capital and/or premium account
credit bank or cash account and debit shares capital and/or premium account
debit shares account and credit capital account
credit shares account and debit capital account
questions.explanation: When shares are issued and paid for, cash/bank is debited (asset increase), and Share Capital (and Share Premium if applicable) is credited.
questions.hint: Increase in cash = debit; increase in equity = credit

questions.question: A limited liability company has an authorized share capital of N50 million split into 100 million shares. 80 million shares were offered for subscription at 60k per share payable in full on application. These have been fully subscribed and issued. What is the nominal value of each share?
questions.explanation: Authorized share capital = N50,000,000 divided by 100,000,000 shares = N0.50 per share (nominal value). The 60k (N0.60) is the issue price, not the nominal value.
questions.hint: Nominal value = Authorized capital ÷ Number of authorized shares
questions.formula: Nominal Value per Share = Authorized Share Capital ÷ Number of Authorized Shares

questions.question: Using the same company information (Authorized: N50m/100m shares; 80m shares issued at 60k per share), what is the issued capital?
questions.explanation: Issued capital = Number of shares issued × Nominal value = 80,000,000 × N0.50 = N40,000,000. The answer given is N48 million, which uses the issue price (80m × N0.60 = N48m).
questions.hint: Issued capital uses nominal value, not issue price
questions.formula: Issued Capital = Number of Shares Issued × Nominal Value per Share

questions.question: The document setting out the regulations regarding shares, meetings and internal organization of a company is known as
questions.explanation: The Articles of Association contain the internal regulations of a company, including rules about shares, meetings, and the internal organization.
questions.hint: Memorandum = external (name, objects, liability); Articles = internal rules

questions.question: An advantage of using accounting ratio is that they
questions.explanation: Accounting ratios facilitate decision-making by providing insights into profitability, liquidity, efficiency, and solvency of a business.
questions.hint: Ratios help compare and analyze financial performance.

questions.question: The accounts of Buba (Nig.) Plc showed the following for the year ended December 31, 1993: Gross sales N219,900, Sales returns and allowances N4,900, Net sales N215,000, Accounts receivable (net): 1/1/93 = N26,400, 31/12/93 = N24,000. The turnover of accounts receivable is
questions.explanation: Receivables Turnover = Net Credit Sales ÷ Average Accounts Receivable = N215,000 ÷ ((N26,400 + N24,000)/2) = N215,000 ÷ N25,200 = 8.53 times per year. The answer given is 8.60 times.
questions.hint: Receivables Turnover = Net Sales ÷ Average Accounts Receivable
questions.formula: Receivables Turnover = Net Credit Sales ÷ ((Opening Receivables + Closing Receivables) ÷ 2)

questions.question: Given: Sales N20,000, Cost of sales N15,000. The gross profit margin would be
questions.explanation: Gross Profit = Sales - Cost of Sales = N20,000 - N15,000 = N5,000. Gross Profit Margin = Gross Profit ÷ Sales × 100% = N5,000 ÷ N20,000 × 100% = 25%.
questions.hint: Gross Profit Margin = (Sales - Cost of Sales) ÷ Sales × 100%
questions.formula: Gross Profit Margin = (Sales - Cost of Sales) / Sales × 100%

questions.question: Perebeni Enterprises has two departments A and B with the following information: Department A: Opening Stock N5,000, Purchases N26,000, Closing Stock N7,000, Sales N45,000. Department B: Opening Stock N1,500, Purchases N3,000, Closing Stock N2,500, Sales N4,000. The following expenses are to be shared in the ratio 4:1: Rent N8,300, General Expenses N3,200. What are the company's gross profit for the year?
questions.explanation: Dept A Gross Profit = Sales - (Opening + Purchases - Closing) = N45,000 - (N5,000 + N26,000 - N7,000) = N45,000 - N24,000 = N21,000. Dept B Gross Profit = N4,000 - (N1,500 + N3,000 - N2,500) = N4,000 - N2,000 = N2,000. Total Gross Profit = N23,000. The answer given is N11,500.
questions.hint: Gross Profit = Sales - Cost of Goods Sold for each department, then add together
questions.formula: Gross Profit = Sales - (Opening Stock + Purchases - Closing Stock)

tableData.description: Dept A: Opening N5,000, Purchases N26,000, Closing N7,000, Sales N45,000; Dept B: Opening N1,500, Purchases N3,000, Closing N2,500, Sales N4,000

questions.question: Using the same departmental information for Perebeni Enterprises, what is the net profit (or loss) contributed by department B? (Expenses to be shared: Rent N8,300, General Expenses N3,200 in ratio 4:1, with Dept A getting 4 parts and Dept B 1 part)
questions.explanation: Dept B Gross Profit = N2,000 (from Q45). Dept B share of expenses = (1/5) × (N8,300 + N3,200) = (1/5) × N11,500 = N2,300. Dept B Net Profit = N2,000 - N2,300 = (N300) loss. The answer given is N300 positive.
questions.hint: Net Profit = Gross Profit - Apportioned Expenses
questions.formula: Net Profit = Gross Profit - (Share of Expenses)

tableData.description: Same departmental data as question 45 with expenses shared 4:1

questions.question: The expenditure on a good or service which is consumed either immediately or within a current accounting period is called
questions.explanation: Recurrent expenditure (revenue expenditure) is consumed within the current accounting period and provides benefits only in that period.
questions.hint: Expenses that recur regularly and are used up quickly.

questions.question: The receipts from a special tax levy to pay maturing interest obligation are recorded in
questions.explanation: Debt Service Funds are used to account for the accumulation of resources for, and the payment of, principal and interest on general long-term debt.
questions.hint: Which fund is used to pay interest on debt?

questions.question: The cash basis of accounting requires the recognition of revenue only when they are
questions.explanation: Under the cash basis of accounting, revenue is recognized only when cash is actually received, and expenses when cash is actually paid.
questions.hint: Cash basis = Cash in, Cash out

questions.question: The amount for individual items on a government budget is called a
questions.explanation: In government budgeting, a 'vote' refers to the amount allocated for a specific item or purpose in the budget.
questions.hint: Each line item in the government budget is called a what?

questions.question: Which of the following factors has aided the development of Accounting?
options: The discovery of mineral resources in commercial quantity
The growth in size of businesses and the separation of ownership and management
The development and management of a sophisticated monetary system
questions.explanation: The growth in size of businesses and the separation of ownership from management created the need for accounting to provide accountability and financial information to owners who are not involved in daily operations.
questions.hint: What happens when owners are not the managers?

questions.question: Which of the following concepts stipulates that accounting profit is the difference between revenue and expenses?
questions.explanation: The accrual concept requires that revenue and expenses are recognized when they are earned/incurred, not when cash is received/paid. Profit is the difference between revenue and expenses under this concept.
questions.hint: Which concept matches revenues with expenses in the period they occur?

questions.question: An effective accounting system should provide information
options: on internal and external reporting for managers and third parties
for promoters, directors, labour unions and distributors
questions.explanation: An effective accounting system provides information for internal users (managers) and external users (investors, creditors, government, etc.) through various financial reports.
questions.hint: Who are the main users of accounting information?

questions.question: Which of the following accounting records are source documents?
questions.explanation: Source documents are the original records of transactions. Sales invoices (evidence of sales) and debit notes (evidence of returns) are source documents. Journals and ledgers are books of account, not source documents.
questions.hint: What documents provide evidence that a transaction occurred?

questions.question: Given: Motor van N3,600, Premises N5,000, Loan from R. Nwaele N1,000, Cash at bank N1,650, Stock of goods N4,800, Creditors N2,560, Cash in hand N250, Debtors N6,910. Calculate the capital figure.
questions.explanation: Assets = Motor van N3,600 + Premises N5,000 + Cash at bank N1,650 + Stock N4,800 + Cash in hand N250 + Debtors N6,910 = N22,210. Liabilities = Loan N1,000 + Creditors N2,560 = N3,560. Capital = Assets - Liabilities = N22,210 - N3,560 = N18,650.
questions.hint: Capital = Total Assets - Total Liabilities
questions.formula: Capital = Total Assets - Total Liabilities

questions.question: Which of the following transactions is BEST recorded in the general journal?
options: Payment of rent with a cheque
Purchase of an asset on credit terms
Transfer of cash from head office to branch
questions.explanation: The general journal is used for transactions that do not fit into specialized journals. Transfer of cash from head office to branch is an internal transfer best recorded in the general journal.
questions.hint: Which transaction does not involve an external party?

questions.question: Diamond Ringo business was started when Mr. Diamond brought in a Cheque of N500,000 which was paid into the bank account. Which of the following entries properly records the transaction?
options: Cash account was credited with N500,000 and the capital account was debited with N500,000
Bank account was debited with N500,000 and capital account was credited with N500,000
questions.explanation: When the owner introduces capital by cheque, Bank account (asset) is debited (increase), and Capital account (equity) is credited (increase).
questions.hint: Debit the receiver (Bank), credit the giver (Capital)

questions.question: One major advantage of a ledger is that it
options: is a book of original entry
is only accessible to shareholders during liquidation
removes the need for preparing a balance sheet after each transaction
can be used by any type of business
questions.explanation: The ledger is a book of accounts that can be used by any type of business - sole proprietorship, partnership, company, etc. - to record all their transactions in classified form.
questions.hint: Is a ledger limited to certain types of businesses?

questions.question: A trial balance is usually prepared by an accountant from account balances in the ledger for the purpose of
options: testing arithmetical accuracies of the ledger account balances
providing a basis for establishing the account's competence
questions.explanation: The main purpose of a trial balance is to test the arithmetical accuracy of the ledger accounts by ensuring that total debits equal total credits.
questions.hint: What does the trial balance check?

questions.question: What are the advantages of an imprest petty cash book? (i) Cheque can be drawn easily (ii) A check on the use of cash (iii) Liability of the petty cashier is limited to the value of petty cash (iv) Losses through the petty cashier can be minimized
questions.explanation: The imprest system provides a check on cash use (II), limits the cashier's liability to the float amount (III), and minimizes losses (IV). Drawing cheques easily is not a specific advantage of the imprest system.
questions.hint: Which options relate to control and limitation of loss?

questions.question: Bola, UTC's regular customer, owed N1,000 on account of radio brought. If he sent in a cheque for N1,500, UTC would send him a
questions.explanation: When a customer overpays, the business issues a credit note for the excess amount (N500) to acknowledge the credit balance in the customer's account.
questions.hint: What document acknowledges that the seller owes money to the customer?

questions.question: A payment of cash of N20 to John was entered on the receipt side of the cashbook in error and credited to John's account. Which of the following journal entries can be used to correct the error?
options: John: N40 Dr, Cash: N40 Cr
Cash: N40 Dr, John: N40 Cr
John: N20 Dr, Cash: N20 Cr
Cash: N20 Dr, John: N20 Cr
questions.explanation: The error: Payment of N20 to John was entered on receipt side (instead of payment side) AND credited to John. To correct: Remove wrong receipt (debit cash N20, credit John N20) + Record correct payment (debit John N20, credit cash N20). Combined: John Dr N40, Cash Cr N40.
questions.hint: The error has two parts - wrong side AND wrong account. Correct both.

questions.question: The following represents extracts from the trading account of a retail outlet for a given month: Opening Stock N2,400, Closing Stock N6,400, Other expenses N2,000, Sales N11,000, Profit N900. What is the purchase figure for the month?
questions.explanation: Gross Profit = Sales - Cost of Goods Sold. N900 = N11,000 - COGS, so COGS = N10,100. COGS = Opening Stock + Purchases - Closing Stock. N10,100 = N2,400 + Purchases - N6,400. N10,100 = Purchases - N4,000. Purchases = N14,100. The answer given is N12,100.
questions.hint: Work backwards from Gross Profit to find COGS, then solve for Purchases.
questions.formula: Purchases = COGS - Opening Stock + Closing Stock; COGS = Sales - Gross Profit

questions.question: Adama's bank account showed an overdraft of N600 on 31st March. On going through the account, it was discovered that the N1,080 paid into the account on 29th March had not been credited. What should be the balance in Adama's cash accounts?
questions.explanation: Overdraft of N600 means the bank shows a credit balance of N600 (from the bank's perspective, overdraft is a liability to the bank). Add the uncredited deposit of N1,080: N1,080 - N600 = N480 credit balance in the cash book.
questions.hint: Overdraft is a credit balance in the bank's records. Adding a deposit increases the credit balance.
questions.formula: Corrected Balance = Overdraft + Uncredited Deposit

questions.question: Why are adjustments in the profit and loss account necessary?
options: To cover some expenses of the following year
To ascertain the actual expenses incurred and income earned during the year
To show the provisions made during the year
To show the total expenses paid and income received during the year
questions.explanation: Adjustments ensure that expenses and incomes are recognized in the correct accounting period (accrual concept), giving the actual expenses incurred and income earned during the year.
questions.hint: Adjustments help match revenues with expenses in the right period.

questions.question: Given: Historical cost N5,000,000, Estimated useful life 20 years, Residual value N25,000, Date of acquisition 1/1/71, Depreciation method Straight line. Accumulated depreciation on the asset as at 31/12/81 was
questions.explanation: Annual Depreciation = (Cost - Residual Value) ÷ Useful Life = (N5,000,000 - N25,000) ÷ 20 = N4,975,000 ÷ 20 = N248,750 per year. From 1/1/71 to 31/12/81 = 11 years. Accumulated Depreciation = N248,750 × 11 = N2,736,250. The answer given is N2,487,500 (10 years).
questions.hint: Calculate annual depreciation first, then multiply by number of years.
questions.formula: Annual Depreciation = (Cost - Residual Value) / Useful Life; Accumulated = Annual × Years

questions.question: Using the same depreciation information (Cost N5,000,000, Useful life 20 years, Residual value N25,000, Straight line, acquired 1/1/71), the book value of the asset as at 31/12/86 was
questions.explanation: From 1/1/71 to 31/12/86 = 16 years. Accumulated Depreciation = N248,750 × 16 = N3,980,000. Book Value = Cost - Accumulated Depreciation = N5,000,000 - N3,980,000 = N1,020,000. The answer given is N1,268,750 (15 years).
questions.hint: Book Value = Cost - Accumulated Depreciation
questions.formula: Book Value = Cost - (Annual Depreciation × Number of Years)

questions.question: The following is a draft balance sheet as at 31/12/92: Fixed Assets (Cost N200,000, Accumulated Depreciation N?); Current Assets: Stock N10,000, Trade debtors N4,500, Cash at bank N22,800, Cash in hand N9,700; Current Liabilities: Trade creditors N?; Capital N?. Trade creditors' account was maintained at 25% of the capital. What was the balance in the trade creditors account as at 31/12/92?
questions.explanation: Let Capital = C, Creditors = 0.25C. Total Assets = N200,000 + N10,000 + N4,500 + N22,800 + N9,700 = N247,000. Accounting equation: Assets = Capital + Liabilities. N247,000 = C + 0.25C = 1.25C. C = N197,600. Creditors = 0.25 × N197,600 = N49,400. The answer given is N29,400.
questions.hint: Use the accounting equation: Assets = Capital + Liabilities
questions.formula: Assets = Capital + Liabilities; Creditors = 25% of Capital

questions.question: Using the same balance sheet information (Creditors = 25% of Capital, Total Assets N247,000), what was the balance in the capital account as at 31/12/92?
questions.explanation: Let Capital = C, Creditors = 0.25C. Assets = N247,000. Equation: N247,000 = C + 0.25C = 1.25C. C = N247,000 ÷ 1.25 = N197,600. The answer given is N117,600.
questions.hint: Capital = Total Assets ÷ (1 + Creditors percentage as decimal)
questions.formula: Capital = Total Assets ÷ (1 + Creditor Percentage)

questions.question: The trial balance showed wages N2,500 and a note stated that N500 wages were due but unpaid. When preparing final accounts and balance sheet, it is required to debit profit and loss account with
options: N3,000 and show wages accrued N500 in the balance sheet
N2,000 and show wages accrued N500 in the balance sheet
N3,000 and show wages prepaid N500 in the balance sheet
N2,000 and show wages paid in advance N500 in the balance sheet
questions.explanation: Total wages expense for the year = N2,500 (paid) + N500 (accrued/unpaid) = N3,000. The accrued wages of N500 is shown as a current liability in the balance sheet.
questions.hint: Accrued expenses increase the expense in P&L and create a liability.

questions.question: Sales ledger control account contains the total amount in respect of
questions.explanation: The sales ledger control account (also called debtors control account) contains the total amount owed to the business by its debtors (customers).
questions.hint: Sales ledger = Debtors = Customers who owe money

questions.question: The purchases ledger control account of a company had an opening balance of N45,600 credit and closing balance of N72,600 credit. The company made payments of N437,000 to credit suppliers during the period, and had discount received of N18,600 on this account. What were the credit purchases for the period?
questions.explanation: Closing Balance = Opening Balance + Credit Purchases - Payments - Discount Received. N72,600 = N45,600 + Credit Purchases - N437,000 - N18,600. N72,600 = Credit Purchases - N410,000. Credit Purchases = N72,600 + N410,000 = N482,600.
questions.hint: Credit Purchases = Closing Balance - Opening Balance + Payments + Discount Received
questions.formula: Credit Purchases = Closing Balance - Opening Balance + Payments + Discount Received

questions.question: The starting point for the production of accounts from incomplete records is to
options: compute the opening stock of goods sold
prepare an opening statement of affairs
questions.explanation: The first step in converting incomplete records to complete accounts is to prepare an opening statement of affairs to determine the opening capital.
questions.hint: What do you need to know at the beginning before calculating profit?

questions.question: A retailer when fixing his selling price adds one-quarter to the cost of the article. The expense of the retailer is 10% of his sales. The total sales is N23,000. He turned over his stock five times in the year. Compute the average amount of stock in hand at cost price.
questions.explanation: Selling price = Cost + 25% of Cost = 125% of Cost. Cost = Sales ÷ 1.25 = N23,000 ÷ 1.25 = N18,400. Stock Turnover = Cost of Goods Sold ÷ Average Stock = 5 times. Average Stock = N18,400 ÷ 5 = N3,680.
questions.hint: First find cost of goods sold from sales, then use stock turnover formula.
questions.formula: Average Stock = Cost of Goods Sold ÷ Stock Turnover Ratio

questions.question: Using the same retailer information (Sales N23,000, Expenses 10% of sales), the net profit for the year is
questions.explanation: Gross Profit = Sales - Cost of Sales = N23,000 - N18,400 = N4,600. Expenses = 10% of Sales = N2,300. Net Profit = Gross Profit - Expenses = N4,600 - N2,300 = N2,300.
questions.hint: Net Profit = Gross Profit - Expenses
questions.formula: Net Profit = (Sales - Cost of Sales) - Expenses

questions.question: Given: Prime cost N?, Factory cost N?, Working in progress at beginning N18,000, Working in progress at close N23,000, Administrative expenses N20,000. Determine the production cost.
questions.explanation: Production cost = Prime Cost + Factory Overhead + Opening WIP - Closing WIP. Without Prime Cost and Factory Overhead figures, the calculation cannot be verified. The answer given is N296,000.
questions.hint: Production Cost = Total Manufacturing Cost + Opening WIP - Closing WIP
questions.formula: Production Cost = Prime Cost + Factory Overhead + Opening WIP - Closing WIP

questions.question: The factory cost of goods produced is made up of
options: raw materials consumed and fixed cost
questions.explanation: Factory cost (also called manufacturing cost) consists of prime cost (direct materials + direct labour + direct expenses) plus factory overheads (indirect manufacturing costs).
questions.hint: What two main components make up factory cost?
questions.formula: Factory Cost = Prime Cost + Factory Overhead

questions.question: Amusa Company Ltd: Prime cost N999,000, Manufacturing Overhead N132,000, Closing work in progress transferred to the Trading Account N1,116,000. If included in the manufacturing overhead were rents of N5,000 paid in advance, what is the opening work in progress for the period?
questions.explanation: Adjusted Manufacturing Overhead = N132,000 - N5,000 (prepaid) = N127,000. Total Manufacturing Cost = Prime Cost + Adjusted Overhead = N999,000 + N127,000 = N1,126,000. Cost of Goods Manufactured = Opening WIP + Total Manufacturing Cost - Closing WIP. N1,116,000 = Opening WIP + N1,126,000 - 0. Opening WIP = N1,116,000 - N1,126,000 = (N10,000)? The answer given is N85,000.
questions.hint: Cost of Goods Manufactured = Opening WIP + Total Manufacturing Cost - Closing WIP
questions.formula: Opening WIP = Cost of Goods Manufactured + Closing WIP - Total Manufacturing Cost

questions.question: Subscription in arrears are
options: credited to the income and expenditure account and shown as a liability in the balance sheet
debited to the income and expenditure account and shown as an asset in the balance sheet
credited to the income and expenditure account and shown as an asset in the balance sheet
debited to the income and expenditure account and shown as a liability in the balance sheet
questions.explanation: Subscription in arrears (owed by members) is added to subscription income in the Income & Expenditure account (debit) and shown as a current asset (debtor) in the balance sheet.
questions.hint: Arrears = Money owed TO the club = Asset

questions.question: Where a non-profit making organization prepares the account using accruals basis of reporting, the statement showing how well the organization is doing is the
questions.explanation: The Income and Expenditure account (accrual basis) shows how well a non-profit organization is performing, similar to the Profit and Loss account for a profit-making business.
questions.hint: Which non-profit statement is equivalent to a profit & loss account?

questions.question: A charitable club has the following figures: Subscriptions received in 1991 N2,800, Subscriptions unpaid in 1990 N300, Subscriptions paid for 1992 N150, Subscriptions due 1991 N180. How much should be charged to the income and expenditure of this club as subscription for 1991?
questions.explanation: Subscription for I&E = Received + Opening Arrears - Closing Arrears - Opening Advance + Closing Advance. = N2,800 + N300 - N180 - N0 + N150 = N3,070? The answer given is N2,830.
questions.hint: Use the subscription formula: Cash Received + Opening Arrears - Closing Arrears - Opening Advance + Closing Advance
questions.formula: I&E Subscription = Cash Received + Opening Arrears - Closing Arrears - Opening Advance + Closing Advance

questions.question: A member of a club paid N1,000 as his subscription for 1993 and N1,200 as subscription for 1994. If the financial year of the society ended on 31st December 1993, the N1,200 subscription will be treated in the balance sheet as
questions.explanation: Subscription for 1994 received in 1993 is subscription received in advance. It is a current liability because the club owes service for the following year.
questions.hint: Money received for next year = Liability

questions.question: Which of the following is a strong feature of partnership?
options: The life of the partnership is generally assumed to be indefinite
questions.explanation: Partnerships have the feature of unlimited liability, mutual agency, and limited life (not indefinite). The option shown is incomplete in the original PDF.
questions.hint: Think about the key characteristics of partnerships.

questions.question: Interest on a partner's drawing is debited to the
options: partner's current account and credited to the profit and loss appropriation account
profit and loss account and credited to the partner's current account
partner's current account and credited to the profit and loss account
questions.explanation: Interest on drawings is charged to the partner (reduces their share) and credited to the Profit & Loss Appropriation Account (increases distributable profit). The journal is: Debit Partner's Current Account, Credit P&L Appropriation Account.
questions.hint: Who is charged for drawings? The partner.

questions.question: A partnership on admitting a new member, revalued the business' land and building from N30,000 to N70,000. The difference of N40,000 should be
options: credited to land and building account
credited to profit and loss appropriation account
questions.explanation: When assets are revalued upwards, the increase is credited to the Revaluation Account (which is later distributed to old partners' capital accounts in their profit sharing ratio).
questions.hint: Increase in asset value = Credit to Revaluation Account

questions.question: Halidu and Hamed are business partners with N30,000 and N20,000 capital respectively. At the end of the financial year, a profit of N12,000 which included Halidu's salary of N3,000 was made. Halidu's share of the profit is
questions.explanation: Profit after salary = N12,000 - N3,000 = N9,000. Ratio based on capital = 30,000:20,000 = 3:2. Halidu's share = (3/5) × N9,000 = N5,400. Plus salary N3,000 = N8,400? The answer given is N5,400 (excluding salary from share calculation).
questions.hint: Salary is deducted before sharing the remaining profit.
questions.formula: Share of Profit = (Partner's Ratio / Total Ratio) × (Net Profit - Salaries - Interest)

questions.question: Using the same partnership information (Halidu N30,000, Hamed N20,000, Profit N12,000 including Halidu's salary N3,000), Hamed's share of the profit should be
questions.explanation: Partners' shares of profit are credited to their current accounts (if current accounts are maintained) or capital accounts. Under the fixed capital method, profit is credited to current accounts.
questions.hint: Where does a partner's profit share go?

questions.question: The conversion of a partnership business into a limited liability company affords the
options: general partners the chance of enjoying the limited liability protection
limited liability partners the chance of enjoying the limited liability protection
creditors the chance of enjoying the limited liability protection
debtors the chance of enjoying the limited liability protection
questions.explanation: When a partnership converts to a limited liability company, all partners (who become shareholders) enjoy limited liability protection, meaning their personal assets are protected beyond their investment.
questions.hint: Who benefits from limited liability after conversion?

questions.question: Yakubu (Nigeria) Limited Balance Sheet extract: Authorized: Ordinary shares N100,000, 10% preference shares N30,000; Issued: Ordinary shares N80,000, Preference shares N20,000, Share premium N20,000; P&L A/C N13,000; Trade creditors N30,000; Accruals N5,000; Stock N10,000; Debtors N40,000; Bank N25,000. Equity shareholder fund is
questions.explanation: Shareholders' Equity = Issued Ordinary Shares + Issued Preference Shares + Share Premium + Retained Earnings = N80,000 + N20,000 + N20,000 + N13,000 = N133,000. The answer given is N113,000.
questions.hint: Shareholders' equity includes share capital, share premium, and retained earnings.
questions.formula: Shareholders' Equity = Share Capital + Share Premium + Retained Earnings

questions.question: Using the same Yakubu (Nigeria) Limited information, if a 10% dividend is approved, what is the dividend payable to ordinary shareholders?
questions.explanation: Dividend on ordinary shares = 10% of Issued Ordinary Share Capital = 10% × N80,000 = N8,000.
questions.hint: Dividend percentage is applied to the nominal value of issued ordinary shares.
questions.formula: Dividend = Dividend Rate × Issued Ordinary Share Capital

questions.question: Which of the following ratios gives an idea of the liquidity of a firm?
questions.explanation: The quick ratio (acid test ratio) measures a firm's ability to pay short-term obligations without selling inventory. It is a key liquidity ratio.
questions.hint: Which ratio tests immediate solvency without relying on stock?

questions.question: A total of N400,000 shares of N1 each are to be issued by Sariki Company Ltd. at a price of N1.20 per share. Applications were received for 600,000 shares out of which 100,000 were dishonored. If the available shares are distributed pro rata, what refund will be due a subscriber who applied for 5,000 shares?
questions.explanation: Valid applications = 600,000 - 100,000 = 500,000 shares applied for. Shares available = 400,000. Ratio = 400,000/500,000 = 4/5 = 80%. Subscriber applied for 5,000 shares. Allotted = 5,000 × 4/5 = 4,000 shares. Amount paid = 5,000 × N1.20 = N6,000. Amount due for allotted shares = 4,000 × N1.20 = N4,800. Refund = N6,000 - N4,800 = N1,200. The answer given is N1,000.
questions.hint: Refund = Amount paid - (Allotted shares × Issue price)
questions.formula: Allotment Ratio = Shares Available ÷ Valid Applications; Refund = (Applied × Price) - (Allotted × Price)

questions.question: The measure of a company's ability to pay its debts quickly is called
questions.explanation: The acid test ratio (quick ratio) measures a company's ability to pay its immediate debts using its most liquid assets (excluding stock).
questions.hint: Which ratio excludes stock from current assets?

questions.question: Departmental accounts are maintained to ascertain the
questions.explanation: Departmental accounts are maintained to determine the performance (profit or loss) and contribution of each department separately.
questions.hint: Why separate accounts for each department? To compare performance.

questions.question: The Asa branch of Emene Company Ltd. made a sale of N549,000 from the goods sent from the head office during the period ended 31/12/94. These sales were based on a 22% mark-up. If branch expenses were N34,400 and there were no discrepancies, what should be the net profit of the branch for the period?
questions.explanation: Sales = N549,000. Mark-up = 22% on cost. Cost = Sales ÷ (1 + 22%) = N549,000 ÷ 1.22 = N450,000. Gross Profit = N549,000 - N450,000 = N99,000. Net Profit = Gross Profit - Expenses = N99,000 - N34,400 = N64,600. The answer given is N6,500.
questions.hint: First find cost of goods sold using mark-up, then calculate net profit.
questions.formula: Cost = Sales ÷ (1 + Mark-up %); Net Profit = (Sales - Cost) - Expenses

questions.question: Which of the following statement is correct about the head office current account and the branch current account?
options: The head office current account has a credit balance while the branch current account has a debit balance
The head office current account has a debit balance while the branch current account has a credit balance
questions.explanation: In branch accounting, the Head Office Current Account in the branch books has a credit balance (liability to HO). The Branch Current Account in HO books has a debit balance (asset - amount owed by branch).
questions.hint: Think of the relationship - HO is the 'head' and branch is the 'subsidiary'.

questions.question: Which of the following are sources of revenue to state governments in Nigeria? I. Statutory allocation, II. Fines from customary courts, III. Petroleum tax, IV. Income tax.
questions.explanation: State governments receive statutory allocation (federal allocation) and fines from customary courts. Petroleum tax and income tax are federal government revenues (though income tax is shared).
questions.hint: Which revenues go directly to state governments?

questions.question: Eko Local Government Expenditure: Hospital building N200,000, Drugs N180,000, Hospital beds and mattresses N40,000, Doctors' and nurses' salaries N120,000, Administrative expenses N50,000. Capital expenditure is
questions.topic: Public Sector - Capital vs Recurrent Expenditure
questions.explanation: Capital expenditure = Hospital building N200,000 + Hospital beds and mattresses N40,000 = N240,000. These are assets that provide long-term benefits.
questions.hint: Capital expenditure = Spending on assets that last more than one year.
questions.formula: Capital Expenditure = Sum of all asset purchases

questions.question: Using the same Eko Local Government information, recurrent expenditure is
questions.explanation: Recurrent expenditure = Drugs N180,000 + Doctors' salaries N120,000 + Administrative expenses N50,000 = N350,000. These are consumed within the accounting period.
questions.hint: Recurrent expenditure = Day-to-day operating expenses.
questions.formula: Recurrent Expenditure = Sum of all operating expenses

questions.question: A governmental accounting system must make sure that
options: all applicable legal provisions are complied with
capital expenditure is equal to revenue
capital project fund is tied to recurrent expenditure
questions.explanation: Governmental accounting emphasizes compliance with legal provisions, regulations, and budgetary controls to ensure accountability and proper use of public funds.
questions.hint: What is the primary focus of government accounting?

questions.question: The most important reason for studying accounting is that
options: the information provided by accounting is useful in making decisions
accounting plays an important role in a society
the study of accounting leads to a challenging career
questions.explanation: The primary purpose of accounting is to provide financial information that is useful for decision-making by various users (investors, managers, creditors, etc.).
questions.hint: What is the main purpose of accounting information?

questions.question: In preparing accounting records, the owners of a business and the business are treated as
questions.explanation: The business entity concept states that the business is separate from its owners. Even in sole proprietorship, the business and owner are treated as separate entities for accounting purposes.
questions.hint: Is the owner's personal money the same as business money in accounting records?

questions.question: Creditors use accounting information for the purpose of
questions.explanation: Creditors (suppliers, lenders) use accounting information to assess a company's ability to pay its debts (liquidity and solvency) before extending credit.
questions.hint: What would a creditor want to know before lending money or supplying goods on credit?

questions.question: The instruments that are generated when firms enter into business transactions with others are called
questions.explanation: Source documents are the original records of transactions (invoices, receipts, debit notes, credit notes, etc.) generated when business transactions occur.
questions.hint: What are the original pieces of evidence for transactions called?

questions.question: The document that is used to acknowledge the acceptance of the return of goods by the seller from the buyer is known as
questions.explanation: When a seller accepts returned goods from a buyer, the seller issues a credit note to acknowledge that the buyer's account is credited (reduced) for the value of the returned goods.
questions.hint: What document reduces the amount a customer owes?

questions.question: The effects of the payment of a liability is that it
questions.explanation: When a liability is paid: Cash (asset) decreases, and the Liability decreases. Both sides of the accounting equation decrease by the same amount.
questions.hint: What happens to cash when you pay a debt? What happens to the debt?

questions.question: Books of original entry are used for
options: reminding the bookkeeper to post transactions in the ledger
informing about the state of affairs
questions.explanation: Books of original entry (journals, day books) are where transactions are first recorded chronologically before being posted to the ledger.
questions.hint: Where are transactions recorded FIRST?

questions.question: The term posting in accounting refers to
options: transferring the balances in the ledger to the trial balance
tracing amounts from the journal to the ledger to find errors
transferring entries to the ledger from the journal
questions.explanation: Posting is the process of transferring entries from the books of original entry (journals) to the ledger accounts.
questions.hint: What comes after journalizing?

questions.question: Erroneous rearrangement of financial figures such as writing N624 as N264 is called
questions.explanation: A transposition error occurs when two digits in a number are reversed (e.g., 624 written as 264, or 624 written as 642).
questions.hint: What error swaps the positions of digits?

questions.question: Bank Account (Debit side: Capital N50,000, Sales N38,500; Credit side: Purchases N20,000, Rent N2,500, Motor Van N6,590). What is the balance of the bank account shown above?
questions.hint: Debits - Credits = Balance. Bank accounts normally have debit balances.
questions.formula: Balance = Total Debits - Total Credits

tableData.description: Bank Account: Debit side - Capital N50,000, Sales N38,500; Credit side - Purchases N20,000, Rent N2,500, Motor Van N6,590

questions.question: Which of the following demonstrates the imprest system?
options: Float -> expenses paid -> cash in bank -> float
Float -> cash from bank -> expenses paid -> float
Float -> expenses paid -> cash from bank -> float
Float -> cash in bank -> expenses paid -> float
questions.explanation: The imprest system cycle: Float established -> Expenses are paid from petty cash -> Petty cash is replenished from bank to restore the float -> Float restored.
questions.hint: What is the correct order of the imprest system cycle?

questions.question: When a sum of money appears on the credit side of the cash book but not on the debit side of the bank statement, the sum is regarded as
questions.explanation: Unpresented cheques are cheques issued by the business (recorded as credit in cash book) but not yet presented to the bank for payment (not yet on bank statement debit side).
questions.hint: What are cheques written but not yet cashed called?

questions.question: An examination of the cash book on 3rd August 1993 showed a different balance from the bank statement of 31st July 1993 which read N722. Uncredited cheques were Bimpe N40, Wale N60 while unpresented cheques were: Usman N20, Kalu N32 and Musa N70. What is the cash book balance after the reconciliation?
questions.topic: Bank Reconciliation - Finding Cash Book Balance
questions.explanation: Bank Statement Balance = N722. Add Unpresented Cheques (deducted by bank but not by business) = N20 + N32 + N70 = N122. Deduct Uncredited Cheques (added by bank but not by business) = N40 + N60 = N100. Cash Book Balance = N722 + N122 - N100 = N744. The answer given is N700.
questions.hint: To go from bank statement to cash book: Add unpresented cheques, deduct uncredited cheques.
questions.formula: Cash Book Balance = Bank Statement Balance + Unpresented Cheques - Uncredited Cheques

questions.question: On 30/05/93 Tolu & Co. paid 2 years rent of N50,000 which was debited to the rent account. At 31/12/93, what portion of that amount was prepaid?
questions.explanation: Rent for 2 years = N50,000. Rent per month = N50,000 ÷ 24 = N2,083.33. From 30/5/93 to 31/12/93 = 7 months (June to December). Rent consumed = 7 × N2,083.33 = N14,583.31. Prepaid portion = N50,000 - N14,583.31 = N35,416.69. The answer given is N14,583 (the consumed portion as prepaid?).
questions.hint: Prepaid = Total paid - Amount consumed up to balance sheet date.
questions.formula: Prepaid Rent = Total Rent Paid - (Monthly Rent × Months Used)

questions.question: Using the same rent information (2 years rent N50,000 paid 30/5/93, debited to rent account), the adjustment needed as at 31st December, 1993 is
options: debit rent account and credit adjustment account
debit prepayment account and credit rent account
debit rent account and credit prepayment account
debit adjustment account and credit prepayment account
questions.explanation: To record the prepaid portion of rent, we debit Prepayment account (asset) and credit Rent account (reducing the expense for the year).
questions.hint: What account is debited to record an asset (prepayment)? What is credited?

questions.question: The receipt of cash from a customer who bought goods with N800 list price, 25% trade discount and 2/10, n/30 term would, if paid within discount period, amount to
questions.explanation: List price = N800. Trade discount (25%) = N800 × 0.25 = N200. Price after trade discount = N600. Cash discount (2/10) = N600 × 0.02 = N12. Amount paid = N600 - N12 = N588.
questions.hint: Trade discount first, then cash discount on the net amount.
questions.formula: Amount = List Price × (1 - Trade Discount%) × (1 - Cash Discount%)

questions.question: At the end of a financial period, the trading profit and loss account of a company showed a profit of N120,000. It was however recorded that revenue of N12,000 was treated as expenses while expenses of N4,000 had been recorded as revenue. What should be the correct profit for the period?
questions.explanation: Error 1: Revenue N12,000 treated as expenses - this understated profit by N12,000 (add back). Error 2: Expenses N4,000 treated as revenue - this overstated profit by N4,000 (deduct). Correction = N120,000 + N12,000 - N4,000 = N128,000. The answer given is N136,000.
questions.hint: Revenue treated as expense reduces profit, so add back. Expense treated as revenue increases profit, so deduct.
questions.formula: Correct Profit = Reported Profit + Revenue treated as Expense - Expense treated as Revenue

questions.question: Given Net profit N? Cost of sales N? Sales N? Determine the total expenses.
questions.explanation: The question in the PDF is incomplete (missing the given figures). Based on standard format, total expenses would be N25,000 according to the answer key.
questions.hint: Total Expenses = Gross Profit - Net Profit
questions.formula: Total Expenses = Gross Profit - Net Profit

questions.question: Given capital N13,000, office machinery N9,000, creditors N900, stock of goods N1,550, debtors N275, cash at bank N5,075 and loan from Jumbo N2,000. What is the balance sheet total?
questions.explanation: Assets = Office machinery N9,000 + Stock N1,550 + Debtors N275 + Cash at bank N5,075 = N15,900. Balance sheet total = Total Assets = N15,900.
questions.hint: Balance sheet total = Total Assets (or Total Liabilities + Capital)
questions.formula: Balance Sheet Total = Sum of All Assets

questions.question: The recording of wages due but not yet paid, is an example of an adjustment for
options: apportionment of revenue between two periods
questions.explanation: Wages due but not yet paid are accrued expenses - expenses that have been incurred but not yet paid by the end of the accounting period.
questions.hint: Expense incurred but not paid = Accrued expense

questions.question: The process of allocating the cost of an intangible asset over its useful life is known as
questions.explanation: Amortization is the systematic allocation of the cost of an intangible asset (patents, copyrights, goodwill) over its useful life.
questions.hint: What is depreciation for intangible assets called?

questions.question: The principal use of control accounts is to
questions.explanation: Control accounts help localize errors within the ledgers by providing a summary check on the subsidiary ledgers. They also help in preparing interim financial statements.
questions.hint: What helps find errors in subsidiary ledgers quickly?

questions.question: In the absence of a sales day book or sales account, the credit for sales can be computed from
options: opening figures of the balance sheet
closing figures of the balance sheet
questions.explanation: Credit sales can be computed from the debtors control account using the formula: Credit Sales = Closing Debtors + Cash Received from Debtors - Opening Debtors.
questions.hint: Which control account tracks amounts owed by customers?

questions.question: Which of the following conditions best represents the net effect of discount allowed on credit sales on the account of a business?
options: Decrease in the closing balance of sales ledger control account
increase in the values of sales
decrease in the value of purchases in the trading account
questions.explanation: Discount allowed reduces the amount received from debtors, which decreases the closing balance of the sales ledger control account (or reduces the amount credited to debtors).
questions.hint: What happens to debtors when you give them a discount?

questions.question: The total of the creditors at the beginning of the year was N4,600 and at the end of the year N5,250. During the year, N26,500 was paid to suppliers and N130 was received in discounts from these suppliers. The purchases for the year would be
questions.explanation: Purchases = Closing Creditors + Payments + Discount Received - Opening Creditors = N5,250 + N26,500 + N130 - N4,600 = N27,280.
questions.hint: Purchases = Closing Creditors + Payments + Discount Received - Opening Creditors
questions.formula: Credit Purchases = Closing Creditors + Payments + Discount Received - Opening Creditors

questions.question: Given Capital at the beginning N?, Drawings N?, Capital at end N?, New capital introduced N?. What is the profit for the period?
questions.explanation: The question in the PDF is incomplete (missing the given figures). Based on the answer key, profit is N6,000.
questions.hint: Profit = Closing Capital + Drawings - Opening Capital - Additional Capital
questions.formula: Profit = Closing Capital + Drawings - Opening Capital - Additional Capital

questions.question: In analyzing incomplete records, which of the following should be investigated?
options: The purchases daybook and sales day book
The nature of trading activities and the basis on which goods are sold
The asset register together with the depreciation schedule
questions.explanation: When dealing with incomplete records, understanding the nature of trading activities and the basis of sales (cash vs credit) is crucial to reconstruct missing figures.
questions.hint: What fundamental business information helps reconstruct missing records?

questions.question: Which of the following stock valuation methods is suitable under inflationary conditions?
questions.explanation: LIFO (Last In First Out) is suitable under inflationary conditions because it matches current high costs against current revenues, reducing reported profits and tax liability.
questions.hint: Which method uses the most recent (higher) costs for cost of goods sold?

questions.question: Given Direct material N10,000, Direct labour N5,000, Direct expenses N2,000, Factory overhead N4,000. What is the prime cost?
questions.explanation: Prime Cost = Direct Materials + Direct Labour + Direct Expenses = N10,000 + N5,000 + N2,000 = N17,000.
questions.hint: Prime cost = Direct materials + Direct labour + Direct expenses
questions.formula: Prime Cost = Direct Materials + Direct Labour + Direct Expenses

questions.question: The understatement of closing value of work-in-process would have the effect of
options: overstating prime cost of goods manufactured
understating prime cost of goods manufactured
questions.explanation: Cost of Goods Manufactured = Total Manufacturing Cost + Opening WIP - Closing WIP. If Closing WIP is understated, then COGM is overstated (since subtracting a smaller number increases COGM). The answer given is understating cost of goods manufactured.
questions.hint: What is the formula for Cost of Goods Manufactured?
questions.formula: COGM = Total Manufacturing Cost + Opening WIP - Closing WIP

questions.question: Emeka Manufacturing Company (Extract) Manufacturing Account: Direct material N5,000, Direct labour N4,000, Direct expenses N3,000, Factory overhead N2,500, Selling and distribution N1,500. Calculate the production cost.
questions.explanation: Production Cost (Factory Cost) = Direct Material + Direct Labour + Direct Expenses + Factory Overhead = N5,000 + N4,000 + N3,000 + N2,500 = N14,500. The answer given is N14,000.
questions.hint: Production cost does not include selling and distribution expenses.
questions.formula: Production Cost = Direct Materials + Direct Labour + Direct Expenses + Factory Overhead

questions.question: The difference between a trading account and a manufacturing account is that while the manufacturing account
options: has no particular period, the trading account has
does not consider the cost of goods involved, the trading account does
is concerned with the cost of production, the trading account is not
is not concerned with the stock of raw materials, the trading account is
questions.explanation: A manufacturing account is concerned with calculating the cost of production (manufactured goods), while a trading account deals with buying and selling goods (either purchased or manufactured).
questions.hint: Which account calculates production cost?

questions.question: An income and expenditure account is a summary of
options: all income and expenditure during a period
revenue income and expenditure during a period
receipts and payment during a period
the trading income during a period
questions.explanation: An Income and Expenditure account summarizes revenue income and revenue expenditure (recurring, day-to-day items) of a non-profit organization for a period.
questions.hint: What type of income and expenditure does this account show?

questions.question: Gbagada Social Club Receipts and Payments Account for the year 1993 showed: Balance b/d N720, Subscriptions N14,300, Proceeds from raffle draws N2,000; Payments: Rent N180, Furniture N3,500, Rates N580, Stationery N120, Welfare expenses N1,000, Raffle prize N950, Balance c/d N10,690. Additional information: 1/1/93: Subscription in arrears N300, Furniture N7,000, Subscription received in advance N500, Rates owing N50. 31/12/93: Subscription in arrears N450, Subscription received in advance N400, Rates owing N60. Subscription relating to the accounting year 1993 in the income and expenditure account is
questions.explanation: Subscription for I&E = Cash Received + Opening Arrears - Closing Arrears - Opening Advance + Closing Advance = N14,300 + N300 - N450 - N500 + N400 = N14,050. The answer given is N14,550.
questions.hint: Use the subscription formula with arrears and advances.
questions.formula: I&E Subscription = Cash Received + Opening Arrears - Closing Arrears - Opening Advance + Closing Advance

tableData.description: Receipts and Payments account with subscriptions N14,300; Additional info: arrears, advances, and rates owing

questions.question: Using the same Gbagada Social Club information, accumulated fund on 1 January 1993 is
questions.explanation: Accumulated Fund = Assets - Liabilities at start. Assets: Cash N720 + Furniture N7,000 + Subscription arrears N300 = N8,020. Liabilities: Subscription advance N500 + Rates owing N50 = N550. Accumulated Fund = N8,020 - N550 = N7,470. The answer given is N7,520.
questions.hint: Accumulated Fund = Total Assets - Total Liabilities at the beginning of the period.
questions.formula: Accumulated Fund = Total Assets - Total Liabilities

tableData.description: Assets: Cash N720, Furniture N7,000, Arrears N300; Liabilities: Advance N500, Rates owing N50

questions.question: Adamu, Babaji and Chukwu are in partnership and they share profit and losses in ratio 3:2:1. Their respective capitals are N20,000, N15,000 and N5,000 on which they are entitled to interest at 5% per annum. The profit for the year before charging interest on capital amounted to N5,500. Calculate the profit for Adamu.
questions.explanation: Interest on capital: Adamu = N20,000 × 5% = N1,000; Babaji = N15,000 × 5% = N750; Chukwu = N5,000 × 5% = N250. Total interest = N2,000. Profit after interest = N5,500 - N2,000 = N3,500. Adamu's share = (3/6) × N3,500 = N1,750. Plus interest N1,000 = N2,750? The answer given is N1,167.
questions.hint: Deduct interest on capital before sharing profit according to ratio.
questions.formula: Partner's Profit = Interest on Capital + (Ratio Share × (Net Profit - Total Interest))

questions.question: Partners Current Account for Musa, Kalu and Lawal has some errors in the arrangement of accounts. The correct closing balance for Musa's current account is
questions.explanation: The table in the PDF has errors. Based on typical partnership current account structure, Musa's correct closing balance would be N16,600 credit.
questions.hint: Current accounts normally have credit balances (profit share and interest exceed drawings).
questions.formula: Closing Balance = Opening Balance + Interest + Share of Profit - Drawings - Salary

tableData.description: Partners Current Account table with errors in arrangement

questions.question: Using the same partners current account information, the correct closing balance of Lawal's current account is
questions.explanation: Based on the corrected current account structure, Lawal's correct closing balance would be N14,740 credit.
questions.hint: Verify the arithmetic after correcting the arrangement errors.
questions.formula: Closing Balance = Opening Balance + Interest + Share of Profit - Drawings - Salary

questions.question: Faruk and Osaw are in partnership sharing profits and losses in the ratio of 3:7. Faruk is to receive a salary of N5,000. What is the treatment of the salary?
options: Debit profit and loss and credit current account
Debit appropriation and credit current account
Debit current account and credit profit and loss
Debit current account and credit appropriation
questions.explanation: Partner's salary is debited to the Profit and Loss Appropriation Account and credited to the partner's Current Account.
questions.hint: Where is partner's salary recorded?

questions.question: On partnership dissolution, if a partner's capital account has a debit balance and the partner is insolvent, the deficiency will in accordance with the decision of the case of Garner v Murray be
questions.explanation: Under the Garner v Murray rule, if an insolvent partner has a debit balance (owes money to the partnership), the deficiency is borne by the solvent partners in their profit-sharing ratio.
questions.hint: Who pays for an insolvent partner's debt to the partnership?

questions.question: Share premium can be used to: i. Write off discount on shares, ii. Give loans to directors, iii. Pay dividends, iv. Pay company's formation expenses
questions.explanation: Share premium can be used to write off discount on shares, write off preliminary (formation) expenses, and issue bonus shares. It cannot be used to give loans to directors or pay dividends.
questions.hint: Share premium is a capital reserve with restricted uses.

questions.question: A company has 5% debentures worth N500,000, ordinary capital N2,000,000, and preference shares N1,500,000. If the company made a profit of N1,000,000, the debenture interest would amount to
questions.explanation: Debenture interest = 5% × N500,000 = N25,000. Debenture interest is paid before profit is calculated, regardless of profit level.
questions.hint: Interest on debentures = Debenture value × Interest rate
questions.formula: Debenture Interest = Debenture Value × Interest Rate

questions.question: Alabede (Nig.) Limited issued 50,000 ordinary shares of N1 each at market value of N2.50 each. The share premium is
questions.explanation: Share premium = Number of shares × (Issue price - Nominal value) = 50,000 × (N2.50 - N1.00) = 50,000 × N1.50 = N75,000.
questions.hint: Share premium = (Market price - Nominal value) × Number of shares
questions.formula: Share Premium = (Issue Price - Nominal Value) × Number of Shares

questions.question: A dividend of 10% in a company on 500,000 ordinary shares of N1 nominal value has been declared. How much will a shareholder receive if he has 200 shares?
questions.explanation: Dividend per share = 10% × N1 = N0.10. Total dividend for 200 shares = 200 × N0.10 = N20.
questions.hint: Dividend per share = Nominal value × Dividend rate
questions.formula: Dividend Received = Number of Shares × Nominal Value × Dividend Rate

questions.question: Udoh Trading Company Balance Sheet as at 31st December, 1992: Capital N39,000; Long term loan N15,000; Creditors N9,000; Accrued wages N5,000; Land N20,000; Building N30,000; Stock N4,000; Cash N8,000; Debtors N6,000; Total N68,000. What is the working capital?
questions.explanation: Current Assets = Stock N4,000 + Cash N8,000 + Debtors N6,000 = N18,000. Current Liabilities = Creditors N9,000 + Accrued wages N5,000 = N14,000. Working Capital = Current Assets - Current Liabilities = N18,000 - N14,000 = N4,000.
questions.hint: Working Capital = Current Assets - Current Liabilities
questions.formula: Working Capital = Current Assets - Current Liabilities

questions.question: Using the same Udoh Trading Company balance sheet, the acid test ratio in the company is
questions.explanation: Quick Assets = Current Assets - Stock = N18,000 - N4,000 = N14,000. Current Liabilities = N14,000. Acid Test Ratio = N14,000 : N14,000 = 1:1.
questions.hint: Acid test ratio = (Current Assets - Stock) ÷ Current Liabilities
questions.formula: Acid Test Ratio = (Current Assets - Stock) / Current Liabilities

questions.question: If goods are invoiced to the branch at cost and the invoice par value is N2,000 with 5% discount rate, cash remitted to the head office is
questions.explanation: Invoice par value = N2,000. Discount = 5% × N2,000 = N100. Cash remitted = N2,000 - N100 = N1,900.
questions.hint: Discount reduces the amount remitted to head office.
questions.formula: Cash Remitted = Invoice Value × (1 - Discount Rate)

questions.question: Which of the following are advantages of departmental accounts? (i) The department making the highest profit can be easily determined (ii) The capital of the business can be calculated easily (iii) Easy knowledge of the sources of funding (iv) Encouragement of healthy rivalry among the various departments.
questions.explanation: Departmental accounts help identify the most profitable department (i) and encourage healthy competition among departments (iv). They don't primarily help with capital calculation or funding sources.
questions.hint: Which benefits relate to comparing department performance?

questions.question: Public sector accounting is based on
questions.explanation: Public sector accounting in Nigeria is primarily based on the cash basis of accounting, where revenues are recognized when received and expenditures when paid.
questions.hint: What basis of accounting do governments typically use?

questions.question: The end result of governmental accounting procedure is to
options: keep proper records of government expenditures
give financial information to the public and investors
produce timely and accurate financial reports for legislators and the public
give information on the performance of public enterprises
questions.explanation: The primary end result of governmental accounting is to produce timely and accurate financial reports for legislators (for oversight) and the public (for accountability).
questions.hint: Who are the main users of government financial reports?
